title: Forecasting Uncertainty
sentences: Meteorologists have grown remarkably skilled, yet public trust in forecasts stays fragile.
A single missed prediction seems to outweigh, in most minds, a hundred accurate ones.
This asymmetry says as much about human psychology as about atmospheric science.
We remember the picnic ruined by sudden rain far more vividly than an ordinary sunny day.
Forecasters, meanwhile, operate within margins of uncertainty the public rarely appreciates.
Probability, after all, is a concept that resists our craving for definite answers.
When a forecast states a modest chance of rain, many people hear only certainty either way.
This gap between statistical nuance and everyday interpretation seems unlikely to close soon.
Perhaps what we truly want from a forecast is not accuracy but reassurance.
Understanding that distinction might do more for our patience than any technological advance.
